**4. Sessions per player per year:**
Mixing dedicated club players (~100+ sessions/yr) with occasional tournament-goers (~10–20/yr), I'll use an average of **~40 sessions per player per year**.

Typical duplicate sessions and tournament rounds run 24–27 boards. I'll use **~25 boards.**

### Calculation

$$
\text{Player-sessions} = 10{,}000 \times 40 = 400{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Table-sessions} = 400{,}000 \div 4 = 100{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Board-plays} = 100{,}000 \times 25 = 2{,}500{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Tricks} = 2{,}500{,}000 \times 13 \approx 32{,}500{,}000
$$

Allowing for some claimed/incomplete hands and rounding the uncertainties, I'll settle on:

## **≈ 31,000,000 tricks**

Typical duplicate sessions run 24–27 boards; team matches can be longer. I'll use **~25 deals/session**.

Table-deals = 7,300,000 ÷ 4 ≈ **1,825,000 table-deals**

**Step 5 — Multiply by tricks per deal**
Tricks = 1,825,000 × 13 ≈ **23.7 million**

Scaling from ACBL (North America: ~150,000 members, roughly 1 billion tricks/year) down to Poland's ~4% share of that player base lands in the 25–40 million range, somewhat higher because ACBL members skew very active. - **Total tricks:** 2,430,000 × 13 ≈ **31.6 million**

## Estimate: ~42,000,000 tricks (42 million)

### Reasoning

**Fixed quantity — tricks per deal:**
Every bridge deal (board) consists of exactly **13 tricks**. In duplicate (tournament) bridge, the same board is replayed at many tables, and each replay is a fresh set of 13 tricks — so I count *table-boards* (one board played once at one table).

**Building up the volume in Poland:**

| Factor | Estimate | Notes |

| Average tables per session | ~10 (≈40 players) | Small clubs ~5 tables, large events 20–40 |
| Boards (deals) per session | ~25 | Typical duplicate session is 24–27 boards |

**Calculation:**

$$
12{,}500 \text{ sessions} \times 10 \text{ tables} \times 25 \text{ boards} = 3{,}125{,}000 \text{ table-boards}
$$

$$
3{,}125{,}000 \times 13 \text{ tricks} \approx 40\text{–}42 \text{ million tricks}
$$

### Cross-check (player-based)
~12,000 regular players × ~45 sessions/year × 25 boards ÷ 4 players/table × 13 tricks ≈ **44 million** — consistent with the above.

### Caveat
This uses the standard convention of **13 tricks per deal**. If you only counted tricks *physically played* (many hands end in a claim), the figure would be roughly 30–40% lower.

**Single point estimate: 42,000,000 tricks.**

### Reasoning

**Key building blocks of a bridge deal:**
- Each deal (board) always contains **13 tricks** (52 cards ÷ 4 players).

|---|---|---|

| **Player-board plays** | **~9,600,000** | 400,000 × 24 |
| Convert to table-board plays (÷4) | ~2,400,000 | 4 players share each board's tricks |
| Tricks per board | 13 | |
| **Total tricks** | **~31–32 million** | |

**The crucial correction:** because 4 players jointly play one set of 13 tricks per board, I divide the player-board figure by 4 before multiplying by 13. (If one mistakenly counts 13 tricks *per player*, the figure inflates fourfold to ~125 million — which would be wrong.)

Also, "claims" (where declarer concedes/claims remaining tricks without physically playing them) might reduce *physically played* tricks by ~10–15%, but I've used the nominal 13 tricks per deal as the standard convention.

$$90{,}000 \times 26 = 2{,}340{,}000 \text{ board-plays}$$

My single best point estimate is **30 million**.

## Estimate: ~40,000,000 tricks

### Reasoning

**Fixed quantity — tricks per deal:**
Every deal (board) of bridge contains exactly 13 tricks (52 cards ÷ 4 cards per trick). I'll use 13 per deal played, ignoring the small reductions from passed-out boards (~1–2%) and claims.

**Key insight — duplicate counting:**
In tournament (duplicate) bridge, the *same* board is replayed at many tables. Each table-play of a board = 4 players experiencing 13 tricks. So the natural unit is the **"board-play"** (one board played once at one table = 13 tricks). 

I'll build up from **player-boards** (boards played per person), then convert:
$$\text{tricks} = \text{player-boards} \times \frac{13}{4} = \text{player-boards} \times 3.25$$

**Estimating total player-boards in Poland:**

Poland is a bridge powerhouse with a large, active scene (PZBS-affiliated clubs, leagues, festivals). I estimate ~10,000 active tournament players, segmented by intensity:

| Occasional | 4,500 | 400 | 1,800,000 |
| **Total** | **10,000** | **~1,230 avg** | **~12,300,000** |

**Converting to tricks:**
$$12,300,000 \text{ player-boards} \times \frac{13}{4} \approx 40,000,000 \text{ tricks}$$

(Equivalently: ~3,000,000 board-plays × 13 ≈ 40 million.)

### Final answer

**≈ 40,000,000 tricks**

## Estimate: **27,000,000 tricks** (≈27 million)

### Reasoning

I'll build this up from the structure of duplicate bridge play.

**Key fact:** Every deal (board) contains exactly **13 tricks**. In duplicate bridge the same deal is replayed at many tables, so the right unit to count is a *"table-board"* = one deal played once at one table = 13 tricks. Each table-board involves 4 players.

**Step 1 — Active tournament players in Poland**
Poland is a bridge powerhouse, but the licensed/active competitive base is modest. I estimate **~8,000 active tournament players** (PZBS-affiliated club and tournament players).

**Step 2 — Sessions played per player per year**
Activity varies enormously (some play 3×/week, many play occasionally). Averaging across the active base, I estimate **~42 sessions/year** (roughly once a week, accounting for a long tail of casual players).

**Step 3 — Boards per session**
Club games and tournament sessions typically run 24–27 boards. I use **~25 boards/session**.

**Step 4 — Combine into player-boards**
$$8{,}000 \times 42 \times 25 \approx 8.4 \text{ million player-boards}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to table-boards (deals actually played at a table)**
Divide by 4 players per table:
$$8.4\text{M} / 4 \approx 2.1 \text{ million table-boards}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 13 tricks per board**
$$2.1\text{M} \times 13 \approx 27.3 \text{ million tricks}$$

**Sanity check:** ~2.1M deals/year ≈ ~5,700 deals/day nationwide — consistent with perhaps a dozen-plus sessions running daily (more on weekends), each with ~10 tables × 25 boards.

### Caveats
- Largest uncertainties are the **player count** (5,000–12,000 plausible) and **sessions/year** (30–60), which together could swing the answer between ~18M and ~45M.
